flashcards.q: What is the direction of magnetic field lines inside a magnet?
flashcards.a: From the South pole to the North pole.

flashcards.q: What does the degree of closeness of field lines represent?
flashcards.a: The relative strength of the magnetic field (closer = stronger).

flashcards.q: True or False: Like magnetic poles attract each other.
What is the shape of magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying wire?
Which rule is used to determine the direction of magnetic field lines around a straight conductor?
In the Right-Hand Thumb Rule, what does the thumb indicate?
flashcards.a: The direction of the electric current.

flashcards.q: In the Right-Hand Thumb Rule, what do the wrapped fingers represent?
flashcards.a: The direction of the magnetic field lines.

flashcards.q: Name the quantity representing the region where magnetic influence can be detected.
Describe the magnetic field lines inside a current-carrying solenoid.
flashcards.a: They are parallel straight lines, indicating a uniform magnetic field.

flashcards.q: Which material is used as a core inside a solenoid to make an electromagnet?
Name the scientist who discovered the magnetic effect of electric current.
What is the unit of magnetic field strength named in honour of the discoverer of electromagnetism?
True or False: Magnetic field lines ever intersect each other.
flashcards.a: False. Intersection is impossible as the needle cannot point to two directions simultaneously.

flashcards.q: Which rule determines the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field?
In Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, what does the forefinger represent?
In Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, what does the middle finger indicate?
In Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, what does the thumb point to?
flashcards.a: Motion or the direction of Force acting on the conductor.

flashcards.q: When is the force experienced by a conductor in a magnetic field maximum?
flashcards.a: When the direction of current is perpendicular to the magnetic field.

flashcards.q: What is the force on a current-carrying conductor if it is placed parallel to the magnetic field?
Name two major organs in the human body where significant magnetic fields are produced.
What does MRI stand for in medical diagnosis?
What is the potential difference between live and neutral wires in Indian domestic circuits?
What is the frequency of the alternating current (AC) supply in India?
Which wire in a domestic circuit has red insulation?
Which wire in a domestic circuit has black insulation?
Which wire in a domestic circuit has green insulation?
What is the purpose of the earth wire?
flashcards.a: Safety measure to prevent electric shocks by providing a path for leakage current.

flashcards.q: When do live and neutral wires come into direct contact?
What is 'overloading' in a domestic circuit?
flashcards.a: A condition where current exceeds circuit capacity due to too many appliances or high-power devices.

flashcards.q: What device is used as a safety component to prevent damage from overloading?
On which principle does an electric fuse work?
flashcards.a: Joule heating effect of electric current.

flashcards.q: True or False: Field lines at the centre of a circular loop appear as straight lines.
How does the field strength change if the number of turns in a coil is increased from 1 to 10?
In a domestic circuit, are appliances connected in series or parallel?
Name the component used to vary the resistance and current in a circuit.
Which wire protects metallic appliances like a refrigerator or toaster from shock?
What is the typical current rating for heavy appliances like geysers and air coolers?
What is the typical current rating for light loads like bulbs and fans?
What happens to a fuse wire when excessive current flows through it?
flashcards.a: It melts due to heating and breaks the circuit.

flashcards.q: Name one device that uses current-carrying conductors and magnetic fields to create motion.
